intent: ADVERSARIAL parser path-decoding: the name= field is HEX-encoded (the auditd encoding for a path with special bytes): 2F746D702F2266696C6520C3A9 = /tmp/"file <e-acute> (embedded double-quote + a UTF-8 multibyte char C3A9). The ausearch companion (denials.ausearch.txt) shows the same path with octal escapes (\303\251) and a literal escaped quote. The parser must decode/pass these path bytes through WITHOUT mangling the SELinux fields, treating the path as opaque enrichment (f4 section 1.3) - it never feeds the TE rule.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). The path bytes are enrichment only; the SELinux fields are clean, so this is a plain TeAllowable read.

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file read;  (audit2allow ignores name= entirely; the encoded path does not affect its output)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: omits the auth_read_shadow() macro (attribute + 5-perm shadow_t padding + 6-perm etc_t:dir). expected.te emits only the single denied `read`.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A parser that naively un-hexes name= and then re-tokenizes the line could re-introduce the embedded quote/space into the field stream and mis-align later fields. A parser that assumes name= is always ASCII may corrupt the UTF-8 C3A9 bytes. The path is opaque enrichment: decode it for display only, never let its bytes feed the rule or break the SELinux-field parse. The TE rule depends only on the tcontext TYPE (shadow_t), never on the object pathname.
